title: Karvonen Heart Rate Zones Calculator 2026 — Max HR & 5 Zones | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Max HR and Training Zones Calculator (Karvonen)
description: HR zones calculator: compute your 5 training zones using the Karvonen formula. Max HR, fat-burning zone, cardio zone. Enter age and resting HR.
intro: **Heart rate zones** let you train at the right intensity for your goal: burn fat, improve aerobic endurance, or raise your VO2max. This calculator uses the **Karvonen formula** (based on heart rate reserve) and the **Tanaka equation (2001)** for max HR, which is more accurate than the outdated '220 − age.' You need to know your **resting HR** (measure it when you wake up, before getting out of bed).
keyTakeaway: **Max HR (Tanaka)**: `208 - 0.7 × age`. **Karvonen**: `target HR = resting HR + % × (max HR − resting HR)`. For a 30-year-old adult with resting HR 65 bpm: fat-burning zone = **137–151 bpm**, cardio zone = **151–165 bpm**.
useCases: You train with a watch/HR strap and want to know what zone you're in.
You want to optimize your training to burn fat (zone 2).
You're preparing for a race and need to define paces by HR zone.
Your cardiologist told you not to exceed a certain HR and you need to calculate the limit.
You're a coach and need to program intensity by zones for your clients.

example.title: Example: 30-year-old with resting HR 65 bpm
example.result: Max HR: **187 bpm**. Fat-burning zone: **138–150 bpm**. Cardio zone: **150–163 bpm**.

explanation:
## The 5 Training Zones

| Zone | % Reserve | Feeling | Main goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z1 (50–60%) | Very easy | Can chat freely | Active recovery |
| Z2 (60–70%) | Easy | Comfortable conversation | Aerobic base, fat burning |
| Z3 (70–80%) | Moderate | Short phrases | Aerobic endurance |
| Z4 (80–90%) | Hard | Only single words | Anaerobic threshold, speed |
| Z5 (90–100%) | Maximum | Can't talk | VO2max, max power |

## Karvonen Formula

```
Target HR = resting HR + percentage × (max HR − resting HR)
```

It's more accurate than using only a % of max HR because it accounts for your **fitness level** (represented by resting HR). An athlete with 45 bpm resting has different zones than a sedentary person with 80 bpm.

## Max HR: Tanaka vs. 220 − age

The old `220 − age` formula overestimates for young people and underestimates for older adults. **Tanaka et al. (2001)** proposed:
```
Max HR = 208 − 0.7 × age
```
Validated with 18,712 participants, it's more accurate across all ages.

## How to Measure Your Resting HR

1. **Upon waking**, before getting out of bed.
2. Lying face up, relaxed.
3. Measure for **60 seconds** (or 15 sec × 4).
4. Do it **3–5 days in a row** and take the average.
5. Don't measure after caffeine, alcohol, or poor sleep.

## Resting HR and Fitness Level

| Resting HR | Level |
|---|---|
| < 50 bpm | Athlete |
| 50–59 | Very good |
| 60–69 | Good |
| 70–79 | Average |
| 80–89 | Below average |
| 90+ | Consult a doctor |

## How Much to Train in Each Zone

For most recreational athletes, the recommended distribution is **80/20**:
- **80% of time** in Zone 1–2 (aerobic base).
- **20% of time** in Zone 4–5 (intervals, tempo).

faq.a: **Zone 2 (60–70% of heart rate reserve)**. At this intensity the body burns a higher proportion of fat as fuel. However, for **total fat loss**, what matters most is the **total caloric deficit**, not the zone.
Not very. It has a ±10–12 bpm error. The **Tanaka formula (208 − 0.7 × age)** is more accurate. Still, the gold standard is a **stress test** with a cardiologist.

faq.q: How do I measure my resting heart rate?
faq.a: Upon waking, before getting out of bed, measure your pulse for 60 seconds. Do it 3–5 days and average. Skip measuring if you slept poorly or drank alcohol.

faq.q: Is training always in a high zone better?
faq.a: No. The **80/20** distribution (80% low zone, 20% high zone) is used by most elite athletes. Always training high leads to overtraining and injuries.

faq.q: Does it work if I take blood pressure medication?
faq.a: **Beta-blockers** significantly reduce max HR. If you take this medication, the calculated zones will be incorrect. Consult your cardiologist for personalized zones.

faq.q: Is it normal for my HR to spike quickly?
faq.a: If your HR rises very quickly with little effort and your resting HR is >80 bpm, it can indicate **low aerobic fitness** or **dehydration**. If persistent with symptoms, consult a cardiologist.
Optical wrist sensors have ±5% error at steady state but fail during exercises with lots of arm movement. **Chest straps** (Polar H10, Garmin HRM) are more accurate.

howToSteps.name: Enter your resting HR
howToSteps.text: Enter your resting heart rate in bpm (measure it when you wake up).

howToSteps.name: Click Calculate
howToSteps.text: Press "Calculate" to get your max HR and 5 training zones.

howToSteps.name: Use the zones in training
howToSteps.text: Configure your watch or HR strap with these zones to train at the right intensity.
